hints.junior: Use @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference) to wrap animations — they only run when the OS reports no motion preference. That's the safe default.
hints.mid: The two strategies: (1) wrap all motion in no-preference — conservative, motion is off by default; (2) write animations normally, override in reduce — motion is on by default, off when requested. Strategy 2 is more common but requires comprehensive reduce overrides.
hints.staff: WCAG 2.1 SC 2.3.3 (AAA): animations triggered by interaction can be disabled. SC 2.3.1 (AA): nothing flashes more than 3 times per second. prefers-reduced-motion is the CSS implementation path for 2.3.3.

hints.junior: Toggle between 'No guard' (always animates) and 'Motion safe' (respects OS preference). If your OS has reduce-motion on, the safe version shows a clean crossfade instead of the slide animation.
hints.mid: In the fixed version, the animation is in a no-preference query — it literally doesn't run if the preference is set. No opacity hack, no JavaScript — just the media query guard.
hints.staff: The reduce override pattern: define animations unconditionally, then target @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) to override with instant transitions. This works but requires discipline — every animation needs a reduce override. The no-preference pattern avoids the maintenance burden.

anecdotes.junior: A user with vestibular disorder reported that our app's page transition made them dizzy. They had reduced motion on in their OS. Our animations ignored it completely. A 4-line fix with no-preference media query resolved it.
anecdotes.mid: The most common miss I see: hero section parallax that ignores prefers-reduced-motion. Parallax is one of the most nauseating effects for vestibular disorder users. It's also one of the easiest to disable with a no-preference guard.
anecdotes.staff: An estimated 35% of users have vestibular disorders to some degree. The prevalence of macOS/iOS reduce-motion OS setting usage is ~30% among users with accessibility settings turned on. This is not a niche: motion sensitivity affects a significant portion of your audience.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're learning how to make animations respectful — by wrapping them in a media query that only runs them when users are OK with motion.
sections.mid: This case covers prefers-reduced-motion in depth: the no-preference vs reduce strategies, the WCAG requirements, and what to do with parallax and page transitions.
sections.staff: We're covering the two CSS strategies with their trade-offs, WCAG 2.1 SC 2.3.3, vestibular disorder prevalence, and the maintenance argument for the no-preference pattern.
